nats-io/nats-server#7847.body:
### Observed behavior

When creating a durable consumer using $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE the server copies the value passed in the `durable_name` field of the consumer config, such that the consumer created has that value in both `durable_name` and in the `name` fields.

When creating a durable consumer using $JS.API.CONSUMER.DURABLE.CREATE however the server doesn't copy the value passed in `durable_name` into the `name` field (which remains empty) for the consumer created.

The issue with this, is that if you create the consumer using either one of the JS API calls, then attempt to create the consumer again (with the same exact configuration, so the consumer creation should be idempotent) but using the other API call then the operation is not idempotent and an error is returned. This is a problem if you want to have application written using different NATS client libraries (some use DURABLE.CREATE others use CREATE) being able to work on the same stream relying on the pattern of 'first create the durable consumer in case it doesn't exist, expecting the consumer creation to not return an error if the durable is already defined (with the same config)).

### Expected behavior

Consumer create should not fail if the consumer is already defined with exactly the same config, regardless of which JS API call was used to create it in the first place. 

### Server and client version

Any server version

### Host environment

_No response_

### Steps to reproduce

Assuming you have a stream 'foo' created, you can emulate a client creating a consumer on the stream using one or the other JS API calls. Create the consumer with one, see how you can do the same request again and it doesn't complain. Then try to create the consumer using the other version, and see how it returns an error that the consumer already exists.

```
nats req '$JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE.foo.test' '{"stream_name":"foo","config":{"durable_name":"test","deliver_policy":"all","ack_policy":"explicit","ack_wait":2000000000,"replay_policy":"instant","max_ack_pending":1,"num_replicas":0,"filter_subjects":[">"]},"action":"create"}'
```

```
nats req '$JS.API.CONSUMER.DURABLE.CREATE.foo.test' '{"stream_name":"foo","config":{"durable_name":"test","deliver_policy":"all","ack_policy":"explicit","ack_wait":2000000000,"replay_policy":"instant","max_ack_pending":1,"num_replicas":0,"filter_subjects":[">"]},"action":"create"}'
```
nats-io/nats-server#7847.title: Discrepancy between $JS.API.CONSUMER.CREATE and $JS.API.CONSUMER.DURABLE.CREATE

nats-io/nats-server#7852.body:
### Observed behavior

JetStream consumers with single subject filter matching all subjects in a stream with high subject cardinality experience severe performance degradation compared to unfiltered consumers.
```
nats bench js consume  --stream=event-stream

NATS JetStream durable consumer (callback) stats: 8,046 msgs/sec ~ 1006 KiB/sec ~ 124.27us

nats bench js consume  --stream=event-stream --filter=event-stream.*.*.*.*

NATS JetStream durable consumer (callback) stats: 235 msgs/sec ~ 30 KiB/sec ~ 4243.87us
```

```
Information for Stream event-stream created 2026-02-19 09:16:11

                Subjects: event-stream.*.*.*.*
                Replicas: 1
                 Storage: File

Options:

               Retention: Limits
         Acknowledgments: true
          Discard Policy: Old
        Duplicate Window: 2m0s
    Allows Batch Publish: false
         Allows Counters: false
       Allows Msg Delete: true
  Allows Per-Message TTL: false
            Allows Purge: true
        Allows Schedules: false
          Allows Rollups: false

Limits:

        Maximum Messages: unlimited
     Maximum Per Subject: 3
           Maximum Bytes: unlimited
             Maximum Age: unlimited
    Maximum Message Size: unlimited
       Maximum Consumers: unlimited

State:

            Host Version: 2.12.4
      Required API Level: 0 hosted at level 2
                Messages: 5,518,269
                   Bytes: 1.1 GiB
          First Sequence: 12 @ 2026-02-19 09:18:13
           Last Sequence: 22,713,937 @ 2026-02-19 10:46:21
        Deleted Messages: 17,195,657
        Active Consumers: 0
      Number of Subjects: 2,179,060

```

### Expected behavior

Filtering for a subject that represents all of messages should have minimal performance impact.

### Server and client version

server version: 2.12.4
client version: 0.3.0

### Host environment

System: Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS (Jammy Jellyfish) 
Kernel: 5.19.0-45-generic
CPU:    Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6140 CPU @ 2.30GHz (72 threads)
Memory: 503,3 GB


### Steps to reproduce

Create stream configured as described in _Observed behaviour_. Use the following script to populate the stream:

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

X=${1:-1000000}

for ((i = 1; i <= X; i++)); do
  d_id=$((RANDOM % 16001))
  da_id=$((RANDOM % 11))
  subject="event-stream.${d_id}.${da_id}.0.0"
  payload="{\"seq\":${i},\"value\":${d_id},\"attribute\":${da_id}}"

  nats pub "$subject" "$payload"
done

echo "Published ${X} messages."

```

run nats bench: 
```
nats bench js consume --nkey=./config/local-nats.nkey --stream=event-stream --filter=event-stream.*.*.*.*

nats bench js consume --nkey=./config/local-nats.nkey --stream=event-stream
```
nats-io/nats-server#7852.title: JetStream consumer subject filtering causes performance degradation even when filter matches all subjects

nats-io/nats-server#7931.body:
### Observed behavior

When I send a lame duck signal to a Windows Service:

```powershell
nats-server.exe --signal ldm=nats-server1
```

Everything looks good in the logs - JetStream shuts down, connections close, "Server Exiting.." appears - but the **process never actually exits**. It just sits there forever in a zombie state.

### Expected behavior

Meanwhile, the regular stop command works perfectly fine:

```powershell
nats-server.exe --signal stop=nats-server1
```

This one terminates the process immediately, as expected.

---

### Server and client version

- **NATS Server**: v2.12.5-RC.2


### Host environment

- **OS**: Windows Server 2019 / Windows 11
- **Running as**: Windows Service
- **Service name**: nats-server1


### Steps to reproduce

1. Install and start as a Windows Service:
   ```powershell
   sc create nats-server1 binPath= "C:\path\to\nats-server.exe -c C:\path\to\config.conf" start= auto DisplayName= "NATS Server 1"
   sc start nats-server1
   ```

2. Check it's running:
   ```powershell
   sc query nats-server1
   ```
   Should show `STATE: RUNNING`

3. Send the lame duck signal:
   ```powershell
   nats-server.exe --signal ldm=nats-server1
   ```

4. Wait a bit (10 seconds if no clients, up to 2+ minutes if there are active clients)

5. Check again:
   ```powershell
   sc query nats-server1
   ```
   **Problem**: Still shows `STATE: RUNNING` even though the server says it's exiting
nats-io/nats-server#7931.title: Windows Service: Lame Duck Mode doesn't exit the process
